By 'dead code' I mean: you cannot cover `else` by tests as every pass through the function would have cleandir set to True. I suppose keeping old test logs could be achieved otherwise by using parametric tmpdir like this one:
https://github.com/aurzenligl/study/blob/master/python-tmpdir/test_x.py
This way one could point to a directory where tmpdirs would be generated in exactly the same fashion as in `/tmp/pytest-of-x` directory. This is how this could look if user gave `/tmp/foo` as the root logs directory:
```
/tmp/foo $ tree -L 1
.
├── logs-109
├── logs-110
├── logs-111
├── logs-112
└── logs-aurzenligl -> /tmp/foo/logs-112
```

Consider changing "logdir" in ini/cmdline option name to "logsdir".
Logdir resembles fixture name which returns path to test-specific dir.
Logsdir resembles name of test session tmpdir's subdir (named "logs") and a couple of identifiers in this module (e.g. self._logsdir or _make_logsdir*).

It would be cleaner to use `usedevelop = true` to ask tox to do a `pip install -e .` for you.  Except I don't know if `pip install -e .` followed by `pip install .[test]` would do the right thing, or if it would undo the -e'ness...

The other non-hacky way of having coverage deal with sources living in .tox/py/*lib/site-packages is to add a `[paths]` section to the .coveragerc, like [zope.securitypolicy](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.securitypolicy/blob/master/.coveragerc#L8-L12) and several other packages already do.  I think this also requires you to run `coverage combine` before you use `coverage report`?

Anyway, I'm not dead set against this, but I think it is hacky and a bit fragile if somebody changes the deps in [testenv].  I'd rather hardcode the `-e .[test]` bit here.  Also, I wonder if it's good or bad to omit `{options}` from install_command -- I don't remember what pip options tox provides in what circumstances.

A summary of ongoing discussion in chat.

These hacks **seem** necessary because the **server** [requires][1] the start key

> The start key must be provided. It can be either closed or open.

and [requires][2] the end key

> The end key must be provided. It can be either closed or open.

I propose that we instead move that validation into the constructor, rather than do it here. This method should just be a "dumb" marshaling of already verified data.

[1]: https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/c5d0b08ecd3049c378972d833a49df99f779f97f/google/spanner/v1/keys.proto#L118
[2]: https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/c5d0b08ecd3049c378972d833a49df99f779f97f/google/spanner/v1/keys.proto#L129

The string returned by the callback is never freed. When `PyObject_CallFunctionObjArgs` returns the reference count for `result` is >=1 and your code has the ownership for that string. In order to let the garbage collector free the string you must do a `Py_DECREF` at some point. 

The problem here is that you can't do it in `yara_include_callback`, because then you are returning to YARA a string that could be freed by the garbage collector at any time. But if you can't do it here, then where?

I have also consider to clear network datasets (`mNetwork`) in `Mle::Stop()`, to ensure the device could refresh its network datasets during reattach process, so then we don't need to check and restart Delay Timer in `SetStateChild()` and `SetStateRouter()`, do you think this way is better?

There is an issue with the way this code is currently structured because of the fact that the callback registered in `SubscribeObjectLocations` may now get called directly. It's possible that right now we will not actually see a bug, but that is only because of the specific ordering of calls made on the object directory by the object manager, and that seems quite brittle. Here is a scenario where I think the code would break:

1. `SubscribeObjectLocations` gets called on objects A and B (e.g., for a `Pull`, or for a different `Wait`). Locations for both are cached in the object directory.
2. `Wait` is called on objects A and B, with 1 object required.
3. **In the same call stack**, `SubscribeObjectLocations` is called on object A. The cached locations are found, this callback fires, and the wait request completes and is erased from `active_wait_requests_`.
4. Again, **in the same call stack**, `SubscribeObjectLocations` is called on object B. `active_wait_requests_.find(wait_id)` will fail (silently).
